name: Razorpay Code Complexity Reviewer
scenario: Razorpay's engineering blog is publishing 'Code Review: Complexity Edition' — a guide showing developers how to identify and fix complexity issues. Build a code complexity analyzer that identifies patterns and suggests improvements.

core.description: Implement 3 versions of 'find duplicates': O(n³) naive (ArrayList.contains inside nested loop), O(n) HashSet. Print complexity for each.
Implement and benchmark string concatenation O(n²) vs StringBuilder O(n). n=5000. Show timing difference.
Analyze a provided 5-line code snippet: sort + loop + HashMap build + scan. Write full step-by-step analysis with labelled Big-O per line. Conclude with total.
Implement and compare naive Fibonacci O(2ⁿ) vs memoized O(n) vs iterative O(n). Verify same results for fib(10)..fib(30).
(4 tests): findDups O(n) faster than O(n³) at n=5000, String O(n) faster at n=5000, fib(30) all methods agree, analysis prints correct Big-O labels.
README: explains 'method calls inside loops' with 3 concrete examples (ArrayList.contains vs HashSet.contains, String += vs StringBuilder).

bonus.description: Group Anagrams: implement O(n×m log m) sorted-key and O(n×m) freq-key. Test with ['eat','tea','tan','ate','nat','bat']. Verify both group correctly.
Merge sort from scratch with comparison counter. Verify at n=1024: comparisons ≈ 1024×10 = 10240 (±20%). Print ratio comparisons/nlogn.
Inversion count: O(n²) brute force + O(n log n) merge sort variant. Verify same count. Benchmark at n=5000.

ai_policy_explanation: 5 hints. Method calls inside loops: analyze EACH method call's complexity. ArrayList.contains = O(n), HashSet.contains = O(1), TreeMap.get = O(log n). Multiply with outer loop. String immutability: result += x creates new String object — copies all existing characters each time. n steps × n characters = O(n²). Use StringBuilder.append() instead — O(1) amortized. Memoization: store results in array/Map, check before recursing. Each of n subproblems solved once = O(n). Recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) solves to O(n log n) — expand tree: n work at each of log n levels.
